1:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt; afterwards he will let you go hence: when he shall let you go, he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether." The witnesses note the last plague announced. On "Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt," the grammatical voices note the last plague. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt; afterwards he will let you go" — God announced to Moses (perhaps in a flashback, or before the final interview) that one last plague would come (the death of the firstborn, vv.4-6), after which Pharaoh would let Israel go; the climax of the judgments; the assurance of the deliverance now at hand.

On "afterwards he will let you go hence," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the deliverance. Calvin and JFB note that after this plague Pharaoh would let them go — the deliverance assured; the end of the contest; the last judgment to break the king's obstinacy.

On "he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the reversal: "he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether" — Pharaoh, so far from refusing, would now thrust Israel out (drive them out in haste, Exodus 12:31-33); the complete reversal (the king who would not let them go now urging them out, "altogether," wholly, all the people and substance); the providence (God's strong hand, Exodus 6:1, forcing the king). The verse displays the last plague announced.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plague — "one plague more upon Pharaoh... afterwards he will let you go," the last plague (vv.4-6, Henry, Gill); "afterwards he will let you go hence," the deliverance (Calvin, JFB); "he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether," the reversal (Exodus 12:31, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the announcing LORD. The verse marks the plague: one more. The verse displays the Pharaoh: to let them go. The verse displays the thrust: out altogether. The verse displays the deliverance: at hand. The announcement displays the last plague and the deliverance. The verse displays the LORD: announcing. The verse displays the plague: the last. The verse displays the Exodus: at hand. The last plague announced displays the deliverance at hand: one plague more, and he shall thrust you out altogether (Exodus 6:1; 12:31-33) — the death of the firstborn to break the king, the complete reversal, the Exodus at hand. The verse displays the spoiling of the Egyptians (vv.2-3).
2:
"Speak now in the ears of the people, and let every man borrow of his neighbour, and every woman of her neighbour, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold." The witnesses note the command to ask the Egyptians for jewels. On "let every man borrow of his neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold," the grammatical voices note the command. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "let every man borrow (ask, request) of his neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold" — God commanded the Israelites to ask of their Egyptian neighbours jewels of silver and gold (and raiment, Exodus 12:35); the "borrow" (Hebrew shaal, to ask, request — not deceitfully borrowing with intent to defraud, but asking as gifts, which the Egyptians freely gave, Exodus 12:36); the spoiling of Egypt foretold (Genesis 15:14, "afterward shall they come out with great substance").

On "borrow of his neighbour," interpreters note the asking. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that "borrow" means "ask" (request) — the Israelites asking, the Egyptians giving (God giving them favor, v.3; Exodus 12:36); not a fraudulent borrowing (the older objection answered) but a free asking and free giving; the just recompense (the wages of long unpaid bondage, the spoiling of the oppressor, Genesis 15:14).

On "jewels of silver, and jewels of gold," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the spoiling: "jewels of silver, and jewels of gold" — the wealth of Egypt given to Israel (the spoiling of the Egyptians, Exodus 12:36; the recompense for the bondage; the substance promised to Abraham, Genesis 15:14); the providence (God enriching his people at the Exodus, the gold and silver later used for the tabernacle, Exodus 35:5). The verse displays the command to ask the Egyptians for jewels.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "let every man borrow of his neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold," the asking (Exodus 12:35, Keil, Gill); "borrow of his neighbour," the asking not defrauding (Exodus 12:36, Calvin); "jewels of silver, and jewels of gold," the spoiling (Genesis 15:14, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanding LORD. The verse marks the command: ask the neighbours. The verse displays the jewels: silver and gold. The verse displays the asking: not fraud. The verse displays the spoiling: foretold. The command displays God enriching his people. The verse displays the LORD: commanding. The verse displays the jewels: to be given. The verse displays the recompense: just. The command to ask the Egyptians for jewels displays the spoiling foretold: ask jewels of silver and gold (Exodus 12:35-36; Genesis 15:14) — the asking and free giving, the just recompense for the bondage, the wealth of Egypt given to Israel. The verse displays the favor given (v.3).
3:
"And the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people." The witnesses note God giving Israel favor and Moses' greatness. On "the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians," the grammatical voices note the favor. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians" — God gave the Israelites favor with the Egyptians, so that they freely gave the jewels (v.2; Exodus 12:36); the providence (God inclining the hearts of the Egyptians to give); the spoiling accomplished by favor, not fraud; the LORD turning the oppressors' hearts.

On "the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note Moses' greatness. Calvin and Ellicott note that Moses was "very great" (held in awe and honor) in Egypt — in the sight of Pharaoh's servants and the people; the greatness of the prophet (the plagues having made his name terrible and honored); the providence (Moses exalted, that his word might prevail).

On "in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the honor: "in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people" — Moses honored by all (even the Egyptians, the servants and the people), though Pharaoh hardened; the awe of the prophet; the providence (the man of God great in the land, the deliverance advancing). The verse displays God giving Israel favor and Moses' greatness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the favor — "the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians," the favor (Exodus 12:36, Keil, Gill); "the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt," Moses' greatness (Calvin, Ellicott); "in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people," the honor (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the favored people. The verse marks the favor: given. The verse displays the Moses: very great. The verse displays the servants: honoring him. The verse displays the providence: at work. The favor displays God's hand enriching and honoring. The verse displays the LORD: giving favor. The verse displays the Moses: honored. The verse displays the Egypt: turned. God giving Israel favor and Moses' greatness display the providence: the LORD gave them favor, and Moses was very great in Egypt (Exodus 12:36) — God turning the oppressors' hearts to give, the prophet honored. The verse displays the death of the firstborn announced (vv.4-6).
4:
"And Moses said, Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt:" The witnesses note Moses announcing the LORD's coming at midnight. On "Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt," the grammatical voices note the announcement. Henry and Gill note that "Moses said, Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt" — Moses (likely still before Pharaoh, ch. 10:29, delivering the final word, or to the people) announced the last plague: about midnight the LORD himself would go out into the midst of Egypt to smite the firstborn (v.5); the solemn announcement of the LORD's coming in judgment; the awful threat.

On "About midnight," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the hour. Calvin and Clarke note the midnight hour (the dead of night, the time of the judgment, Exodus 12:29) — the appointed time; the solemnity of the midnight stroke; the terror of the night.

On "will I go out into the midst of Egypt," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the LORD's coming: "will I go out into the midst of Egypt" — the LORD himself coming in judgment (going through Egypt, by the destroying angel, Exodus 12:23; the immediate hand of God); the solemnity (God himself the executor of the last plague); the providence (the judgment that would break Pharaoh and free Israel). The verse displays Moses announcing the LORD's coming at midnight.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the announcement — "Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt," the last plague (v.5, Henry, Gill); "About midnight," the hour (Exodus 12:29, Calvin, Clarke); "will I go out into the midst of Egypt," the LORD's coming (Exodus 12:23, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the announcing Moses. The verse marks the announcement: thus saith the LORD. The verse displays the midnight: the hour. The verse displays the LORD: going out. The verse displays the judgment: at hand. The announcement displays the LORD's coming in judgment. The verse displays the Moses: announcing. The verse displays the midnight: appointed. The verse displays the firstborn: to be smitten. Moses announcing the LORD's coming at midnight displays the last judgment: about midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt (Exodus 12:23, 29) — the LORD himself coming to smite the firstborn, the solemn midnight stroke. The verse displays the death of the firstborn (vv.5-6).
5:
"And all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sitteth upon his throne, even unto the firstborn of the maidservant that is behind the mill; and all the firstborn of beasts." The witnesses note the death of the firstborn announced — the crux of the last plague. On "all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die," interpreters expound the judgment. The reformed and the dominant reading note the great threat — "all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die": the tenth and last plague — every firstborn in Egypt to die (the eldest son of every family, the hope and stay of the house; and the firstborn of the beasts); the immediate, universal stroke of death (by the LORD's own hand, the destroying angel, Exodus 12:23, 29); the judgment on Egypt's firstborn (a recompense, the witnesses note, for Pharaoh's slaying of Israel's sons, Exodus 1:22; and Israel being God's firstborn, Exodus 4:22-23, "let my son go... if thou refuse, I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn"); the climax of the judgments.

On "from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sitteth upon his throne, even unto the firstborn of the maidservant," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the universality. Wesley and Ellicott note the sweep of the judgment — from the highest (Pharaoh's firstborn, the crown prince, the heir) to the lowest (the maidservant's firstborn, behind the mill); none exempt (the great and the mean alike); the universal stroke (the leveling judgment of God, no rank or station spared).

On "and all the firstborn of beasts," the grammatical-historical note the extent: "and all the firstborn of beasts" — even the firstborn of the cattle smitten (the judgment on the beasts the Egyptians worshipped, Exodus 12:12, "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment"); the complete judgment; the providence (the last plague breaking Pharaoh, freeing Israel, ch. 12). The verse displays the death of the firstborn announced.

The witnesses agree the substance: every firstborn in Egypt, from Pharaoh's to the maidservant's, and of the beasts, was to die (Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the last plague: "all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die" — the tenth and climactic judgment, the eldest of every house smitten by the LORD's own hand (Exodus 12:23, 29), a recompense for Pharaoh's slaying of Israel's sons (Exodus 1:22) and the threatened doom for refusing God's firstborn (Exodus 4:22-23); the universal stroke, from the highest to the lowest, none exempt, and the firstborn of the beasts and Egypt's gods judged (Exodus 12:12). The verse displays the firstborn-death crux. The verse marks the firstborn: all to die. The verse displays the sweep: Pharaoh to the maidservant. The verse displays the beasts: firstborn smitten. The verse displays the judgment: the last plague. The death displays the climactic judgment on Egypt. The verse displays the firstborn: doomed. The verse displays the LORD: the smiter. The verse displays the Israel: God's firstborn. The death of the firstborn announced displays the crux: all the firstborn shall die, from Pharaoh's to the maidservant's (Exodus 4:22-23; 12:12, 29; 1:22) — the climactic judgment, the recompense for the slaying of Israel's sons, the doom for refusing God's firstborn. The verse displays the great cry (v.6).
6:
"And there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more." The very brief verse notes the great cry of Egypt foretold. The grammatical voices note the cry. Ellicott and Gill note that "there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more" — the death of the firstborn (v.5) would raise a great cry (of grief and anguish) throughout Egypt, unequalled before or after; the universal mourning (every house bereaved, Exodus 12:30); the terror and grief of the judgment; the unequalled lamentation.

On "a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the mourning. Clarke and Ellicott note the great cry (the universal wail of grief, every house having its dead, Exodus 12:30) — the anguish of the judgment; the terror of the bereaved land.

On "such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more," the grammatical-historical note the unequalled: "such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more" — the cry unequalled (the greatest mourning Egypt ever knew); the magnitude of the judgment; the providence (the cry that would break Pharaoh's obstinacy, Exodus 12:31). The verse displays the great cry of Egypt foretold.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the cry — "a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it," the mourning (Exodus 12:30, Ellicott, Gill); "a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt," the anguish (Clarke, Ellicott); "such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more," the unequalled (Exodus 12:31, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mourning land. The verse marks the cry: great. The verse displays the Egypt: bereaved. The verse displays the mourning: unequalled. The verse displays the judgment: terrible. The cry displays the universal grief of the judgment. The verse displays the cry: rising. The verse displays the Egypt: in anguish. The verse displays the Pharaoh: to be broken. The great cry of Egypt foretold displays the unequalled mourning: a great cry, such as never was nor shall be (Exodus 12:30) — the universal anguish of the bereaved land, breaking the king. The verse displays the distinction of Israel (v.7).
7:
"But against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast: that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel." The witnesses note God's difference between Egypt and Israel. On "against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue," the grammatical voices note the safety. Ellicott and Gill note that "against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast" — while Egypt mourned its dead, Israel would be so safe that not even a dog would bark (move his tongue) against them, man or beast; the perfect safety and peace of Israel (in the night of Egypt's judgment); the proverbial expression of complete security (not the least disturbance); the distinction of God's people.

On "shall not a dog move his tongue," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the peace. Clarke and Gill note the figure ("not a dog move his tongue") — the perfect quiet and safety of Israel (not a sound of trouble); the peace of God's people in the night of judgment; the complete protection.

On "that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the distinction: "that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel" — the purpose: that all might know the LORD distinguishes between his people and the world (as the flies, Exodus 8:22; the darkness, Exodus 10:23); the distinction of grace (Israel safe, Egypt judged); the providence (God severing his own in the day of judgment, Malachi 3:18; the sealing of his people, Revelation 7:3). The verse displays God's difference between Egypt and Israel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the safety — "against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue... that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference," the distinction (Exodus 8:22, Ellicott, Gill); "shall not a dog move his tongue," the peace (Clarke, Gill); "that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel," the distinction (Malachi 3:18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the distinguished Israel. The verse marks the safety: not a dog stir. The verse displays the peace: complete. The verse displays the difference: God's. The verse displays the distinction: of grace. The safety displays God's people kept in the night of judgment. The verse displays the Israel: safe. The verse displays the Egypt: judged. The verse displays the LORD: distinguishing. God's difference between Egypt and Israel displays the distinction of grace: not a dog shall move his tongue against Israel; the LORD putteth a difference (Exodus 8:22; 10:23; Malachi 3:18) — the perfect safety of God's people in the night of the world's judgment. The verse displays the servants bowing (v.8).
8:
"And all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out, and all the people that follow thee: and after that I will go out. And he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger." The witnesses note Moses foretelling the Egyptians' submission, and his departure in anger. On "all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out," the grammatical voices note the submission. Ellicott and Gill note that "all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out, and all the people that follow thee" — Moses foretold that Pharaoh's own servants would come and bow before Moses, begging Israel to depart (Exodus 12:31-33); the reversal (the proud court forced to entreat the deliverers); the urgency of the Egyptians to be rid of Israel after the last plague.

On "and after that I will go out," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the departure. Calvin and Wesley note Moses' word ("after that I will go out") — Israel to depart after the plague and the Egyptians' entreaty; the deliverance at hand; the Exodus foretold.

On "he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the anger: "he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger" — Moses left Pharaoh in great (holy, righteous) anger (indignation at the king's obstinacy and hardened defiance); the just wrath of the prophet (not sinful passion, but holy indignation at the contempt of God); the providence (the last interview ended, the plague to fall, ch. 12). The verse displays Moses foretelling the submission and his departure in anger.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the submission — "thy servants shall come down... and bow down... saying, Get thee out... and after that I will go out," the reversal (Exodus 12:31, Ellicott, Gill); "and after that I will go out," the departure (Calvin, Wesley); "he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger," the holy anger (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departing Moses. The verse marks the submission: the servants bowing. The verse displays the entreaty: get thee out. The verse displays the departure: of Israel. The verse displays the anger: holy. The foretelling displays the reversal and Moses' indignation. The verse displays the Moses: foretelling. The verse displays the servants: to bow. The verse displays the anger: great. Moses foretelling the submission and his departure in anger display the reversal and holy indignation: thy servants shall bow and beg us to go; and Moses went out in great anger (Exodus 12:31-33) — the proud court to entreat the deliverers, the prophet's holy wrath at the king's defiance. The verse displays the purpose of the hardening (v.9).
9:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you; that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt." The witnesses note the purpose of Pharaoh's refusal — the multiplying of God's wonders. On "Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you," the grammatical voices note the refusal. Gill and Clarke note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you" — God told Moses that Pharaoh would not hearken (would persist in refusing); the foreknown obstinacy (the hardening confirmed, Exodus 7:3-4); the king's persistent defiance foretold.

On "that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt," interpreters expound the purpose. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the purpose — "that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt": Pharaoh's refusal (his hardening) was overruled by God for the multiplying of his wonders (the many signs and plagues, the great displays of his power); the providence (God's purpose in the obstinacy — that his works might be multiplied, his power shown, Exodus 7:3-4; 9:16; Romans 9:17, "that I might shew my power in thee"); the sovereign overruling (the king's defiance serving God's glory, the deliverance made the more glorious by the many wonders); the lesson — God turns even the obstinacy of the wicked to his own ends and glory.

On the purpose, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the wonders: the multiplying of the wonders (the many plagues, the signs told to generations, Exodus 10:2) — God's power displayed the more by the prolonged contest; the providence (the obstinacy overruled for the glory of God and the instruction of his people). The verse displays the purpose of Pharaoh's refusal.

The witnesses agree the substance: Pharaoh would not hearken, that God's wonders might be multiplied (Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the purpose of the refusal: "that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt" — Pharaoh's foreknown obstinacy (Exodus 7:3-4) overruled by God for the multiplying of his signs and the displaying of his power (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17), the deliverance made the more glorious, his works told to generations (Exodus 10:2); God turning the obstinacy of the wicked to his own glory. The verse displays the multiplied-wonders purpose. The verse marks the refusal: Pharaoh not hearken. The verse displays the purpose: wonders multiplied. The verse displays the power: shown. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The purpose displays God overruling the obstinacy for his glory. The verse displays the Pharaoh: refusing. The verse displays the wonders: multiplied. The verse displays the glory: of God. The purpose of Pharaoh's refusal displays the multiplying of God's wonders: Pharaoh shall not hearken, that my wonders may be multiplied (Exodus 7:3-4; 9:16; Romans 9:17) — the obstinacy overruled for the displaying of God's power and glory. The verse displays the summary of the signs (v.10).
10:
"And Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh: and the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land." The witnesses note the summary of the signs and Pharaoh's hardening. On "Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh," the grammatical voices note the signs. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh" — the summary of the whole contest: Moses and Aaron had done all the signs and wonders (the nine plagues, the rod-serpent) before Pharaoh; the recapitulation of the judgments; the faithful execution of God's commission; the wonders all wrought.

On "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the hardening. Calvin and Keil note "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart" (the judicial hardening, the summary of the hardening through the plagues, Exodus 4:21; 7:3; following the king's self-hardening, Exodus 8:15, 32) — the divine sovereignty over the obstinate king; the hardening that occasioned the multiplied wonders (v.9).

On "he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land," the grammatical-historical note the refusal: "he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land" — the persistent refusal (through all the plagues); the obstinacy (until the last plague, ch. 12, would force the release); the providence (the hardening overruled, the wonders multiplied, the deliverance made glorious); the summary before the Passover and Exodus (ch. 12). The verse displays the summary of the signs and Pharaoh's hardening.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summary — "Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh: and the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the recapitulation (Keil, Gill); "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the hardening (Exodus 4:21, Calvin, Keil); "he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land," the refusal (ch. 12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the summary of the contest. The verse marks the wonders: all done. The verse displays the hardening: by the LORD. The verse displays the refusal: persistent. The verse displays the Passover: to come. The summary displays the whole contest and the hardening. The verse displays the Moses and Aaron: faithful. The verse displays the Pharaoh: hardened. The verse displays the Exodus: at hand. The summary of the signs and Pharaoh's hardening display the whole contest: Moses and Aaron did all the wonders, and the LORD hardened Pharaoh (Exodus 4:21; 7:3) — the judgments wrought, the king obstinate, the deliverance now at hand (ch. 12). The verse closes the chapter, before the Passover (ch.12).
